title: Taping & Wrapping Basics
summary: Learn why athletic tape and elastic wraps are used, the core patterns behind them, and the safety checks that keep a taped or wrapped limb safe.

sections.heading: Why we tape and wrap
body: Athletic tape and elastic wraps show up constantly on the sideline, but they serve different jobs. Taping uses rigid or semi-rigid tape to limit a joint's range of motion at the end range where an injury tends to happen — for example, restricting the ankle from rolling into the inward-turning position that causes most ankle sprains.

Wrapping uses stretchy elastic material to apply even compression, hold an ice pack or padding in place, or provide light, non-restrictive support. The two are often combined: tape to control motion, an elastic wrap over the top to add compression and hold everything together.

This unit covers taping and wrapping at the recognition level — knowing the purposes, principles, and safety checks. Hands-on application (actually wrapping a basket weave or a compression spiral) is a skill you build through supervised practice, not from reading alone.

sections.heading: Three purposes, three tools
body: Almost every taping or wrapping job is built around one or more of these goals:
list: Support — restricting a joint's motion at the extreme range where it gets injured, without locking it completely (rigid athletic tape, e.g., closed basket weave for the ankle).
Compression — applying even pressure to control swelling or hold a cold pack against the skin (elastic wrap, e.g., a compression spiral after an acute sprain).
Protection — cushioning or shielding a healing area, or securing padding over a bruise or old injury (tape, wrap, or padding combined, e.g., a foam donut taped over a bruised area).

sections.heading: Skin prep and general taping principles
body: Good tape jobs start before the tape ever touches skin. Clean the area of sweat, dirt, and lotion, and shave excessive hair — tape pulls painfully and won't stick well over hair or oily skin.

Apply a thin, even layer of tape adherent (a sticky spray) to help the tape grip, then a layer of pre-wrap (a thin foam underwrap) to protect the skin from tape irritation and make removal less painful. Heel-and-lace pads or foam can go under pre-wrap over areas of friction, like the front of the ankle.

As you apply tape, keep strips smooth and overlapping by about half their width, and never let tape wrinkle or bunch — especially over bony prominences like the malleoli (ankle bones), where a wrinkle can dig in and cause a blister or a pressure sore within minutes of activity.

sections.heading: Ankle taping: the closed basket weave
body: The closed basket weave (sometimes called a 'closed basketweave' or gibney) is the classic supportive ankle taping pattern, used to limit inversion (the foot rolling inward) after a mild sprain or as prophylactic support for an athlete with a history of sprains.

In words, the pattern builds in this order: two anchor strips (one around the lower leg above the ankle, one around the arch of the foot) to give later strips something to stick to. Then alternating 'stirrups' (vertical strips running up one side of the ankle, under the heel, and up the other side) and 'horseshoes' (horizontal strips around the ankle, low to high) are layered one at a time, each overlapping the last by about half its width, working from the inside of the ankle first since inversion is what you're limiting.

The weave finishes with closing strips called 'figure-8s' and 'heel locks' that wrap diagonally across the top of the foot and around the heel to lock the whole structure together, followed by a few more circumferential strips to smooth and secure the edges.

image.description: Step diagram of a closed basket weave ankle taping job on a right ankle, shown as a build-up sequence: anchor strips (one around the lower leg, one around the arch), alternating stirrups (vertical strips up one side, under the heel, up the other) and horseshoes (horizontal strips low to high), and finishing figure-8 and heel-lock strips crossing diagonally over the foot and around the heel. Use numbered layers or a light color gradient to show build order. Clean instructional-illustration style.
image.alt: Step diagram of the closed basket weave ankle taping pattern, showing anchors, stirrups, horseshoes, and finishing figure-8/heel-lock strips.

sections.heading: Elastic wraps: spirals and figure-8s
body: Elastic wraps (sometimes called compression bandages) are applied differently depending on the goal. A spiral wrap — overlapping circular turns moving up a limb — is the standard pattern for even compression, such as wrapping a lower leg after an ankle sprain to control swelling or holding an ice pack in place.

The non-negotiable rule for any compression wrap: always wrap distal to proximal — start at the point farthest from the heart (for example, the toes or the foot) and spiral upward toward the body. Wrapping the other direction can trap fluid in the far end of the limb and make swelling worse instead of better.

A figure-8 pattern — crossing diagonally back and forth, like the number 8, over a joint such as the ankle, knee, or shoulder — is used when you need the wrap to hold its position across a bending joint without slipping, which a simple spiral tends to do over a joint.

sections.heading: After you wrap: the circulation check
body: A wrap that is 'snug' is good; a wrap that is 'tight' is a problem. Every compression wrap needs a circulation check within a few minutes of application and again if the athlete reports new symptoms.

Check capillary refill: press a toenail or fingernail bed (whichever is distal to the wrap) until it turns white, release, and count how long the pink color takes to return — normally under 2 seconds. Also ask the athlete directly whether they feel any numbness, tingling, or increasing pain, and look at skin color for pale, blue, or dusky tone and at temperature for coolness compared to the other limb.

Any of these findings — capillary refill slower than 2 seconds, numbness or tingling, pale/blue/cool skin, or increasing throbbing pain — means the wrap is too tight. Unwrap it immediately and reapply with less tension. When in doubt, redo it looser; you can always add another layer, but you cannot un-cut off circulation after the fact.

image.description: Close-up of a hand pressing on a toenail bed distal to a wrapped ankle, with a small two-step sequence: step 1 shows the nail turning white under pressure, step 2 shows the nail released with a stopwatch icon reading 'under 2 seconds' as color returns. Clean instructional-illustration style.
image.alt: Diagram of the capillary refill check: pressing a toenail bed until white, then timing how quickly pink color returns.

callout.title: Field tip: check, don't guess
callout.text: Never assume a wrap is safe just because it looks neat. Build the capillary refill and numbness/tingling check into your routine every single time — it takes 15 seconds and it's the difference between a supportive wrap and a dangerous one.

sections.heading: When NOT to tape or wrap
body: Taping and wrapping are tools for managing an already-evaluated, minor issue or for supporting a joint during return to play — they are not a way to avoid evaluating an injury.

Taping is not a substitute for evaluation and rehabilitation. It can support a joint during activity, but it does nothing to heal a ligament, rebuild strength, or restore balance — an athlete who relies on tape instead of doing rehab exercises is setting up for a worse re-injury down the road.

callout.title: Do not tape or wrap over these situations
callout.text: Never tape or wrap: an acute injury that has not yet been evaluated by a supervising professional, a suspected fracture or dislocation, an open wound or active bleeding under where the tape would sit, or a joint an athlete wants taped specifically to hide pain and keep playing. In all of these, stop, remove the athlete from activity, and refer for evaluation first.

sections.heading: Reassessing and re-doing a tape job
body: Tape and wraps loosen with sweat, movement, and time — a job that was snug at the start of a game may be dangerously loose or, less often, may have tightened uncomfortably by the end. Re-check support and circulation at halftime or between periods for any athlete taped for an injury history.

Redo a tape job any time it has loosened enough to lose its supportive purpose, has slipped out of position, has become wet enough to lose adhesion, or if the athlete reports new numbness, tingling, or pain. There is no penalty for retaping — there is real risk in leaving a job that no longer does its job.

quiz.question: What is the main purpose of rigid athletic taping on a joint like the ankle?
choices: Restrict motion at the end range where injury tends to occur
Apply even compression to reduce swelling
Hold an ice pack in place against the skin
Completely immobilize the joint so it cannot move at all
quiz.explanation: Taping is a supportive tool: it limits motion at the extreme range that caused the injury (like inversion at the ankle) without locking the joint completely. Compression and holding ice are jobs for elastic wraps, not rigid tape.

quiz.question: You are applying an elastic wrap to a swollen ankle and lower leg. Which direction should you wrap?
choices: Proximal to distal — start at the knee and spiral down toward the foot
Distal to proximal — start at the foot and spiral up toward the knee
Direction doesn't matter as long as it's snug
Start at the middle of the calf and wrap outward in both directions
quiz.explanation: Compression wraps always go distal to proximal (far from the heart toward the body). Wrapping proximal to distal can trap fluid in the foot and make swelling worse instead of pushing it out.

quiz.question: Which finding tells you a compression wrap needs to be removed and reapplied looser?
choices: The wrap looks neat and even
The wrap was applied distal to proximal
Capillary refill of the toenail takes longer than 2 seconds to return to pink
The athlete says the wrap feels snug
quiz.explanation: Slow capillary refill (longer than about 2 seconds) means blood flow is restricted and the wrap is too tight. A snug, neat, correctly-directioned wrap is exactly what you want — refill time is what tells you if tension has gone too far.

quiz.question: An athlete asks you to tape their wrist tightly so the coach won't notice it hurts and pulls them from the game. What should you do?
choices: Tape it as requested since the athlete knows their own body best
Tape it loosely as a compromise
Wrap it instead of taping, since wrapping is gentler
Decline, and have the wrist evaluated before any taping or return to play
quiz.explanation: Taping to hide an unevaluated injury and keep an athlete in the game is one of the clearest 'do not tape' situations. The wrist needs evaluation first — taping is never a way to mask pain from a supervising professional.

quiz.question: What is pre-wrap used for in a taping job?
choices: A thin foam underwrap layer that protects skin and eases tape removal
The final layer that locks the whole tape job together
A spray that increases tape's stickiness
An elastic bandage applied for compression after taping
quiz.explanation: Pre-wrap is the thin foam layer applied over adherent and before tape, protecting the skin from irritation and making the tape easier to remove later. Tape adherent (the sticky spray) is a separate step applied directly to skin.

quiz.question: In the closed basket weave for ankle taping, why do wrinkles over the malleoli (ankle bones) matter so much?
choices: Wrinkles make the tape less waterproof
A wrinkle over a bony prominence can dig in and cause a blister or pressure sore quickly
Wrinkles only affect how the tape job looks, not function
Wrinkles increase how much the ankle can invert
quiz.explanation: Bony prominences have little soft tissue padding, so a wrinkle concentrates pressure right against bone and skin — it can raise a blister or sore within one practice. Smooth, overlapping strips avoid this.

quiz.question: Which pattern is best suited to keep an elastic wrap from slipping as a joint like the knee or ankle bends?
choices: Simple straight spiral with no crossing
A single circular loop at the joint only
Figure-8 pattern crossing diagonally over the joint
Wrapping only above the joint, never across it
quiz.explanation: A figure-8 crosses diagonally over the joint, which anchors the wrap through movement in a way a plain spiral cannot. Spirals work well on straight limb segments but tend to slide at a bending joint.

quiz.question: A tape job applied at the start of practice looks loose by the second half. What is the correct move?
choices: Leave it, since removing tape mid-practice is not allowed
Add athletic tape on top of the loose job without removing anything
Switch the athlete to an elastic wrap only, permanently
Redo the tape job — a loose tape job has lost its supportive purpose
quiz.explanation: Tape loosens with sweat and movement, and a loose supportive tape job no longer restricts the motion it's meant to limit. Retaping is quick, low-risk, and expected — there's no reason to leave a job that isn't doing its job.

flashcards.back: Support (limit motion at injury-prone range), compression (control swelling, hold ice/padding), protection (cushion or shield an area).
Taping = rigid/semi-rigid tape, restricts a joint's range of motion. Wrapping = stretchy elastic material, applies compression or holds padding/ice.
The rule for compression wraps: start farthest from the heart (e.g., toes/foot) and spiral upward toward the body, to push swelling out rather than trap it.
Classic ankle taping pattern: anchors, then alternating stirrups (vertical) and horseshoes (horizontal), finished with figure-8s and heel locks to limit inversion.
Overlapping circular turns moving up a limb, distal to proximal — the standard elastic wrap pattern for even compression.
Diagonal crossing pattern (like the number 8) used over a bending joint (ankle, knee, shoulder) so the wrap holds position instead of slipping.
Thin foam underwrap applied after tape adherent and before tape — protects skin and makes tape removal less painful.
Sticky spray applied to clean, dry skin before pre-wrap/tape to help the tape grip and stay in place through activity.
Clean skin of sweat/dirt/lotion, shave excess hair, apply adherent, then pre-wrap — poor prep means tape won't stick and can irritate skin.
Press a nail bed until white, release, and time the return of pink color. Normal is under 2 seconds; slower means a wrap is too tight.

flashcards.front: Signs a wrap is too tight
flashcards.back: Capillary refill over 2 seconds, numbness or tingling, pale/blue/cool skin, or increasing throbbing pain — unwrap and reapply looser immediately.

flashcards.front: When NOT to tape or wrap
flashcards.back: Unevaluated acute injuries, suspected fracture/dislocation, open wounds/active bleeding under the tape, or taping to hide an injury and keep an athlete playing. Taping is never a substitute for evaluation and rehab.
